tam.subtitle: Total ang bao cash circulating at SG weddings annually — the gift-flow pool that could in theory be redirected to IPC charity.
tam.total_equation: 22,955 marriages × ≈70% banquet × ≈225 guests × ≈S$280 avg ang bao

stacks.name: SG citizen marriages 2024
stacks.source: SingStat Statistics on Marriages and Divorces 2024 (released 2025-07-07)

stacks.name: Banquet-hosting share × guest count × ang bao
stacks.source: SingaporeBrides + Seedly + Her World 2024-2025 wedding banquet & ang bao guides

inputs.label: Avg guests/wedding
inputs.value: ~225 (10 tables of 10 at hotel, lower at restaurant)

stacks.result_label: ≈S$1.01B gross ang bao pool, of which ~10% addressable to charity = S$102M TAM

sam.subtitle: Couples + guests for whom 250% IRAS tax relief is materially attractive AND Altru's PayNow-native couple-led product fits.
filters: Couples with household income SGD 100K+ (ICP top half)
Chinese / Western / inter-ethnic couples (ang bao-cash convention dominant)
Guests with taxable income to use 250% relief (IRAS resident filers)
Couple willing to redirect 100% (charity-only) or 50% (split) of gift flow

stacks.name: ICP couple subset
stacks.source: SingStat household income 2024 + Seedly hotel banquet rate guides

som.subtitle: Realistic 3-year cumulative donation GMV given 2-3 person team, organic-led GTM, hotel/planner partnerships ramping in year 2.
som.total_equation: Yr1 100 + Yr2 350 + Yr3 750 weddings × ~S$2k charity GMV/wedding

stacks.name: 3-year wedding ramp
stacks.source: Internal capacity model anchored to SG citizen marriage base of ~22,955/yr
stacks.result_label: ≈S$2.4M cumulative GMV × 5% platform fee = ~S$120K cumulative platform revenue

implications.headline: TAM is vertical-narrow, not VC-scale — capital plan must match
implications.body: S$102M TAM and S$32M SAM cap a SG-only wedding-charity model below seed/early-A scale. Plan should be lean (founder-led, hotel/planner partnerships) until SAM expands via SEA cross-border or category extensions (e.g. funerals, milestones). Avoid burn-heavy paid growth; the math doesn't support it.

implications.headline: Year-3 GMV target = S$1.5M, ~750 weddings ≈ 3% of SG cohort
implications.body: SOM of S$2.4M / 3 yrs implies year-3 run-rate ~S$1.5M GMV at ~750 weddings. That's 3% of SG citizen marriages — ambitious but plausible if hotel partnerships land. Agent 3 should size the 5% platform fee against this volume; couple-side ARPU is ~S$100/wedding, not subscription.

implications.headline: Adjacencies (funerals, milestone gifting, SEA expat) unlock 2-3x SAM
implications.body: Same PayNow-IPC-tax-relief mechanic extends to baby showers, milestone birthdays, funeral wakes (white-packet), and SG-resident expat weddings. Agent 4 should treat these as Wave 2 attack plans — a wedding-only niche caps at S$32M SAM.

implications.headline: Couples will not pay subscription — 5% take-rate must do all the work
implications.body: ICP wedding is a one-time event. There's no recurring revenue per couple. Agent 3 should not propose a Plus/Pro tier subscription; the 5% platform fee is the unit economic. Question to test: can a 3-5% take-rate on average S$2K GMV/wedding sustain a team — or do we need adjacent monetisation (charity featured listings, hotel partnership fees, planner referral)?

implications.headline: Religious-community SAM is parallel, not stacked
implications.body: Mosque (MUIS), Hindu temples (HEB), and church wedding-tithing flows operate in their own community channels with their own tax-relief paths. They are NOT part of Altru's primary 250%-IRAS-IPC SAM. Agent 4 should size the secondary religious segment as a separate ICP rather than pretend it's reachable from the primary product.

market_size.methodology_appendix:
TAM derivation: SG citizen marriages 2024 = 22,955 (SingStat, released July 2025; 5.7% YoY decline from 24,355 in 2023; 5-year average ~23,000). Banquet-hosting share assumed ~70% based on culture (most Chinese, inter-ethnic, and Western SG weddings include a hotel/restaurant reception; Malay and some Indian weddings use void-deck or community venues at lower spend). Average guest count ~225 (a 10-table hotel banquet seats ~100, but mid-to-large weddings host 25-30 tables = 250-300 guests; we centre on 225 as cohort mean). Average ang bao 2025: SingaporeBrides 2025 guide cites S$200 for 4-star hotels through S$300+ at Marina Bay Sands / Capella / Raffles / St Regis, with S$280 a reasonable cohort mean. Multiplied: 22,955 × 70% × 225 × S$280 ≈ S$1.01B total ang bao GMV. Of that, ~10% is realistically redirectable to a wedding-charity platform like Altru in the next 3 years (the rest stays in the traditional couple-keeps-cash convention) → TAM = S$102M.

SAM filters down to ICP: (a) household income SGD 100K+ couples, who skew toward hotel/restaurant banquets and have taxable income worth optimising via 250% relief; (b) Chinese, Western, inter-ethnic banquet weddings where ang bao-cash is the dominant gift convention (Malay-only and small Indian weddings have lower cash-gift density); (c) guests with taxable income to use 250% relief — limits SAM to non-foreign-resident, non-zero-income guests. Combined filter-share ≈45% of TAM. Charity-redirect propensity layered on top: ~15% of ICP couples will go full-charity (no cash for couple) and ~30% will offer split (charity + cash to couple). Net SAM ≈ S$32M.

SOM grounded in capacity: founder-led GTM in year 1 (100 weddings via early-adopter outreach + hotel pilots), expanding to 350 in year 2 (planner partnerships, paid social), 750 in year 3 (hotel rev-share). Avg charity GMV per wedding ~S$2,000 reflecting that most adopters do partial-redirect, not full-redirect, of S$50-100K of theoretical ang bao flow. 3-year cumulative GMV S$2.4M, platform revenue at 5% ≈ S$120K cumulative. Sensitivity: doubling year-3 wedding count to 1,500 (≈6% cohort share) lifts SOM to ~S$5M GMV and ~S$250K cumulative revenue — still sub-Series-A scale, supporting the 'lean and patient' capital recommendation.

No published TAM exists for SG wedding-charity specifically; this triangulation is bottom-up from SingStat marriage counts × SingaporeBrides ang bao norms. Cross-checked against Commissioner of Charities total IPC donations (FY2023: S$3.55B per COC Annual Report 2023), of which weddings could plausibly contribute ~3% incrementally if widely adopted — consistent with TAM order of magnitude.

sources.title: SingStat Statistics on Marriages and Divorces 2024 (released 2025-07-07)
Seedly Wedding Ang Bao Hotel Venues 2024-2025 Guide

policies.title: IRAS 250% tax deduction for IPC donations — extended to 31 Dec 2026
policies.body: For every SGD 1 donated to an IPC for local-benefit causes, SGD 2.50 is deducted from the donor's taxable income (Year of Assessment following the donation year). Donor must provide NRIC/FIN/UEN at point of donation; the IPC files the record with IRAS and the deduction is auto-applied. Scheme was extended to 31 Dec 2026 in Budget 2023 and is currently the active rate for 2024, 2025, and 2026 donations. Beyond 31 Dec 2026 the scheme requires legislative renewal (default would revert to 100% deduction).
policies.implication_for_us: The 250% rate is Altru's single most load-bearing structural advantage — it is what makes the math work for the guest. A guest at the 22% personal-tax bracket donating SGD 200 saves SGD 110 (250% × 22% × 200) and the charity gets ~SGD 190 (after 5% Altru fee). At a 100% rate the saving falls to SGD 44 — the entire pitch weakens. CRITICAL: lobby/advocate for renewal beyond 2026-12-31 starting Q3 2026, and build a 'sunset risk' contingency (couple-side messaging that emphasises emotional impact, not tax math, as fallback). Agent 4 should make '250% IRAS relief' a primary canvas dimension; Agent 3 must front-load growth into 2026 while the rate is locked in.

policies.title: Commissioner of Charities IPC criteria — governing eligibility for tax-deductible status
policies.body: Only registered IPCs may issue IRAS-recognised tax-deduction receipts. IPC status is granted by sector administrator (e.g. NCSS for social services, MOH for health, MOE for education) under the Charities Act, requires public-benefit purpose, governance compliance, and Annual Returns to the Office of the Commissioner of Charities. ~600+ IPCs registered nationally; the registry is searchable on charities.gov.sg. Total IPC donations received FY2023: SGD 3.55 billion (COC Annual Report 2023, 20% above FY2021).
policies.implication_for_us: Altru must integrate against the official IPC list, not the broader charity list. ~600 IPCs is enough breadth that couples will find a cause they care about; the 'IPC charity catalog' is a positive UX surface, not a constraint. Agent 4 should treat 'IPC charity breadth' as a canvas dimension where Altru ties Giving.sg (the incumbent national platform) but beats every wedding registry (zero IPC integration). Recruit 50-100 IPCs as launch partners by 2026-Q4 to anchor SEO/discovery; prioritise emotionally resonant categories for couples (children, animals, elderly, mental health, cancer).

policies.title: MAS Payment Services Act — expanded scope effective 2024-04-04
policies.body: Amendments to the Payment Services Act (PSA) took effect 4 Apr 2024, expanding regulated payment activities and adding user-protection rules. PayNow-rail merchant collections (which Altru uses to route donations) remain subject to standard PSA notices, including PSN01 (AML/CFT) and merchant on-boarding requirements. Donation collection via PayNow is generally permitted but the platform operator is on the hook for KYC/AML on the charity-side recipient. No surcharge on PayNow customers permitted (ABS rule).
policies.implication_for_us: Altru sits in a PSA grey zone: it doesn't custody guest funds (PayNow flows direct to charity UEN) but does operate payment-collection software for IPCs. Agent 3/4 should engage MAS for an informal categorisation early; if Altru is deemed a 'merchant acquisition service' it remains exempt, if it's deemed a 'payment account issuance' it requires a Major Payment Institution license (~SGD 100K capital). The platform fee (5%) must be billed to the couple/IPC — NEVER as a donor surcharge — to comply with ABS no-surcharge rule. Bake this into product copy now.

policies.title: Singapore Budget 2025 — SG Gives Matching Grant + S$600M philanthropy push
policies.body: Announced Feb 2025, Budget 2025 committed S$600M+ in matching funds across 3 programmes including the new SG Gives Matching Grant (S$250M), additional Community Chest matching, and expanded Philanthropy Tax Incentive Scheme (PTIS) coverage. Designed to catalyse new entrants and broaden the donor base, especially for community/social-service IPCs.
policies.implication_for_us: The matching mechanic (typically S$1 gov per S$1 donor) effectively doubles the impact-per-dollar story Altru can tell guests. A guest's S$200 ang bao becomes S$400 to the IPC plus S$110 in personal tax savings — a ~2.5x impact narrative. Agent 4 should bake 'SG Gives matching multiplier' into the per-donation receipt UX and into couple-acquisition copy ('your wedding could unlock S$X in matching grants'). Lobby IPC partners to opt into matching schemes where applicable. This is a material wedge against US wedding registries which have no equivalent.

policies.title: PDPA + PDPC AI Guidelines — donor data handling in charity tech
policies.body: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) governs collection, use, and disclosure of donor personal data; non-compliance penalty up to S$1M or 10% of SG revenue. PDPC released AI Advisory Guidelines (1 Mar 2024) and Children's Personal Data Guidelines (28 Mar 2024). Charities/IPCs are obligated to publish a privacy policy, appoint a DPO, and protect donor records (NRIC, donation history, receipt details). PayNow + IRAS receipt flow requires NRIC capture — high-risk PII.
policies.implication_for_us: Altru handles guest NRICs at scale (every donor gives one for the IRAS receipt) — this is hot PII. Agent 4/5 should treat 'PDPA-grade NRIC handling' as a non-negotiable trust signal: encrypt at rest, never display on couple dashboards, purge after IRAS submission window. Appoint a DPO (can be a director, SGD 0 cost) and publish a public privacy policy in v1. A breach here ends the company — trust-led brand cannot recover. Budget S$5K-10K for a PDPA audit before public launch.

policies.title: Charities (IPC) Regulations — tax-deduction receipts and Annual Returns
policies.body: IPCs must issue tax-deductible receipts (TDRs) to donors providing NRIC/FIN/UEN. From 2011 onward, IPCs must submit donation records electronically to IRAS at year-end (Annual Return of Tax-Deduction Donations). IRAS applies the 250% deduction automatically to donor's tax assessment based on these submissions. No paper receipt needed for the donor.
policies.implication_for_us: The auto-receipt-via-IRAS flow is Altru's killer-feature wedge over wedding registries: zero post-event admin for the donor or couple. Agent 4 should make 'auto IRAS receipting' a canvas dimension and feature it as a primary couple-side benefit ('your guests don't lift a finger to claim relief'). Build the IPC-side automation (CSV/API export to IRAS file format) as a P0 — this saves IPC partners ~5-15 hours/event of admin and is the single most concrete reason an IPC says yes to integrating with Altru.

cultural_signals.observation: Ang bao is treated as the couple's recoupment of wedding cost, not a free gift — redirecting it to charity requires careful framing or guests perceive it as 'making them pay for something they don't get'.
cultural_signals.evidence: Mothership SG (2025-12) reported a SG groom's S$200 'max ang bao' stance as controversial precisely because most SG guests expect ang bao rates to peg to wedding-package cost; SingaporeBrides 2026 guide explicitly calculates 'cover-the-cost' baseline at hotel-tier prices (S$200 4-star → S$300+ luxury). Seedly 2024-2025 guide framed ang bao as 'a contribution to the couple's wedding expense'. The cultural contract: guest covers their plate cost + small surplus for the couple's nest egg.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Altru's positioning must NOT be 'donate instead of giving the couple money' — that breaks the social contract. Reframe as 'we already had everything we need; choose your cause' or 'each plate is sponsored — your gift goes to X charity in our names'. Test couple-side onboarding language with 5+ couples before launch. Agent 4: 'cultural reframing' is a content/positioning whitespace, not a feature whitespace — the feature is identical to a charity platform; the win is in the language.

cultural_signals.observation: Hotel-tier dictates ang bao rate and rate-shaming is real — luxury hotels (MBS, Capella, Raffles, St Regis) command S$300-450/guest; 4-star venues S$200-250.
cultural_signals.evidence: SingaporeBrides Wedding Ang Bao Rates 2026 published a hotel-by-hotel rate table; Her World Singapore 2025 guide and PreciousFilms 2025 guide cite the same hotel-tier banding. Seedly 2024-2025: 'rates have risen 10-20% in 2024 due to wedding-package inflation'. Marina Bay Sands and luxury venues skew $300-450; mid-tier hotels S$220-280.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Altru's average donation ticket size will be hotel-tier-dependent. Couple-side dashboard should show realistic 'expected ang bao pool' calculator anchored to their venue (e.g. 'Marina Bay Sands, 250 guests → ~S$80,000 ang bao pool, ~S$40,000 redirectable to charity at 50% split'). Agent 3 should build venue-tier-aware projections; Agent 5 should treat 'venue-aware ang bao calculator' as a high-value findability dimension.

cultural_signals.observation: Tea ceremony ang bao operates separately from banquet ang bao — only family elders give at tea, only wedding guests give at banquet.
cultural_signals.evidence: Bridely SG Tea Ceremony Guide 2026, EmceeCheng Tea Ceremony Guide 2026, and Merry Bees Wedding Tradition guides all confirm two separate ang bao occasions. Tea ceremony ang baos are smaller (often jewellery instead) and from elders/family. Banquet ang baos are from extended guests at standardised market rates.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Altru should target the BANQUET ang bao flow exclusively in v1 — tea-ceremony ang bao is family-private and not a fit for a public PayNow QR flow. Agent 4: messaging must distinguish the two so couples don't confuse tradition (tea → family elder → cash) with the new product (banquet → guest → QR → charity). Build couple-onboarding FAQ that explicitly addresses this concern; we expect this to be the #1 family-elder objection.

cultural_signals.observation: Cultural ang bao norms diverge sharply by community: Chinese cash-in-red, Malay cash-in-green at lower amounts (S$20-70 typical), Indian cash-or-card-in-envelope, Western mixed.
cultural_signals.evidence: ExpatLiving SG Wedding Etiquette Guide; EmceeServices SG ang bao guide; Justmarriedfilms angbao guide. Malay weddings: void deck S$20-30/pax, country club S$30-50, hotel S$50-70 — markedly lower than Chinese hotel rates. Indian wedding gifts often non-monetary (gold/sarees) plus envelope cash. SG inter-ethnic marriages were 36% of citizen marriages in 2023 (Mothership).
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Primary Altru ICP is Chinese, Western, and inter-ethnic SG couples — NOT the Malay cohort, where ang bao rates are too low to support the unit economics (S$50 ang bao × 5% fee = S$2.50; ticket-level transaction costs eat margin). Agent 4 should treat religious-community channels (MUIS for Malay, HEB for Hindu, churches for Christian wedding-thanksgiving) as ADJACENT segments with their own product surfaces, not core Altru.

cultural_signals.observation: Wedding planners are gatekeepers but largely pen-and-paper on gifting — most do not pre-recommend a registry/charity flow, and they earn commission from venues, not from gifting platforms.
cultural_signals.evidence: OneThreeOneFour, Blissful Brides, Bridal Club SG (per Altru's own competitor scan) are all SG planner/vendor platforms; none integrate gifting; no SG planner offers charity-routing as a standard menu item per public site review (2026-04). Industry margin model is venue/vendor commissions (10-15%) and bundled deals — no incentive to push a gifting platform unless commissioned.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Planner channel exists but requires explicit rev-share to activate. Agent 4: design a planner-referral programme (e.g. SGD 100/wedding referral fee or 0.5% of GMV) and build a planner-side dashboard. Don't expect organic planner adoption — they have zero current incentive. SECONDARY channel: hotel banquet sales teams. They CAN be the wedge: couples ask hotels 'what do you have for charity options' and the hotel having Altru as a default integration is high-leverage. Pursue 3-5 hotel pilots.

cultural_signals.observation: SG couples 28-40 increasingly values-driven; Gen Z adopters favour smaller, purpose-led 'micro' weddings, ESG investing, and brands aligned with their values.
cultural_signals.evidence: Deloitte 2024 Gen Z & Millennial Survey (SEA): purpose-driven careers and consumption are top 3 values. Maybank2u SG 2024-2025 reaching-purpose-led-consumers: 82% Gen Z and 72% young millennial interested in ESG investing vs 69% average. Rosette Designs SG 2025 Gen Z wedding trends: 'micro-weddings, purposeful spending, smaller guest lists, deeper meaning'. CNN Business Feb 2024: ~50% couples adjusting weddings due to costs, 'eliminating gifts and favors' a known trend.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The narrative tailwind is real: SG couples 28-40 in Altru's ICP are PRECISELY the cohort already opting out of consumerist registries. Agent 4: position Altru as the natural wedding-gifting product for values-led couples — not a charity pitch, but a way to express identity. Agent 5: hero copy should lead with 'a wedding your values can be proud of' or similar, NOT with 'tax-deductible donations'. Tax math is the second-order proof, not the first-order promise.

cultural_signals.observation: 62% of SG residents donate to charity annually; 19% give through informal channels — the giving habit is broad but the channel split is fragmented.
cultural_signals.evidence: NVPC National Giving Study 2023 (published Jun 2024): 62% donor participation, 19% via informal groups, ~30% volunteer rate. Same study notes 'amount per giver dropped' between 2021 and 2023 — elasticity downward. CAF World Giving Index 2024: SG ranks top-3 globally on generosity.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The supply of willing donors is enormous; the constraint is reaching them at the right moment. A wedding is a high-emotion, high-attendance, high-public-commitment moment — the perfect catalyst. Agent 4: 'right moment' is the wedge against Giving.sg (always-on, low-emotion) and direct PayNow (no narrative). Build couple-side stories ('our wedding raised S$25K for X') as social-proof ammo; expect strong UGC/PR potential.

economic_signals.implication_for_us: TAM is shrinking at the top of the funnel — fewer SG weddings each year (low TFR, late marriage). Altru cannot rely on a growing market; growth must come from share-take within the existing cohort, expansion to adjacent occasions (funerals, milestones), or SEA cross-border (SG-MY couples). Agent 3 must not project linear TAM growth; Agent 4 should sequence Wave 2 adjacencies sooner rather than later.

economic_signals.indicator: SG median monthly household income (resident employed households)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Household income is rising in real terms — ICP couples (HHI SGD 100K+) are a growing cohort despite total marriage count declining. Wedding spend can absorb a 5% platform fee at hotel-tier ang bao volumes (avg S$80K ang bao pool → S$4K platform fee on full redirect = noise). Agent 3: pricing pressure on the couple-side fee is low; the 5% take-rate is sustainable.

economic_signals.value: +10-20% YoY in 2024 packages (per SingaporeBrides + Seedly); 2025 expected +5-10% as labour and food costs moderate; ang bao rates pegged to package cost
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Wedding cost inflation is direct tailwind for Altru: as banquet prices rise, ang bao expectations rise, and the redirect-to-charity narrative gets MORE compelling ('we've already spent S$80K, we don't need another S$50K — give back instead'). Agent 4: position cost-inflation as a positive trigger event in couple-side messaging.

economic_signals.value: 0.7% in 2025 (down from 2.8% in 2024); CPI All-Items 0.9% in 2025
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Disinflation eases discretionary-spend pressure on guests — ang bao amounts likely hold or rise modestly in 2026. The 'cost of living' objection (often raised by elder guests reluctant to give larger ang baos) loses sting. Agent 3: don't model an elasticity hit from inflation — the macro is benign at present.

economic_signals.indicator: SG total IPC donations FY (Commissioner of Charities)
economic_signals.value: S$3.55B in FY2023 (+20% vs FY2021's S$2.95B per COC reports)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Underlying SG charitable giving is at all-time high and growing 8-10% YoY. If Altru captures even 1% of incremental giving growth (S$30M/yr at current run-rate), that's larger than its entire SAM — affirming the model is undersized vs the macro pool. Agent 4: highlight 'tide rising' macro in pitch context; donors are flowing, the only question is the channel.

economic_signals.value: 500M FAST transactions in 2024, S$661.7B value (+31% volume / +36% value YoY); 68% of SG Gen Z prefer PayNow
economic_signals.implication_for_us: PayNow is the default rail for SG digital P2P/P2M payments and adoption is still growing 30%+ YoY. Altru's PayNow-native architecture is the right rail; building on cards or alternative wallets would be wrong. Agent 4: 'PayNow native' is a hard advantage vs US wedding registries (Honeyfund, Zola, MyRegistry, The Knot) which require credit-card USD checkout. Agent 5: ensure mobile UX (camera → PayNow QR scan → amount → NRIC auto-fill → send) is sub-30-second.

economic_signals.indicator: SG total charitable giving (NVPC National Giving Study)
economic_signals.value: 62% donor participation rate in 2023; 19% via informal channels; ~30% volunteer rate
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Donor-side adoption barrier is low — nearly two-thirds of SG residents already donate. The product doesn't need to convert non-donors; it just needs to redirect existing givers' next decision (the wedding ang bao). Agent 3: budget low on donor-side acquisition; cost-of-acquisition is on the COUPLE side, where the propensity-to-redirect must be unlocked.

adoption_patterns.note: Altru's category is best understood as 'wedding-charity transactional platforms' — a near-greenfield niche. We re-interpret the SME/MNC fields as 'couple penetration' and 'corporate/institutional penetration'. Couple penetration: fewer than 1% of SG citizen weddings currently route gifts to charity via a dedicated platform. Anecdotal: a small number of couples per year use Giving.sg fundraising pages or print PayNow QR codes for a chosen IPC. No platform-tracked 'wedding-charity' GMV exists in SG today. Corporate/institutional penetration: 0% — no wedding registry today is operated as a corporate philanthropy vehicle. Caveat for SEA: this is a SG-only number; cross-border adoption (e.g. SG-MY couples) is even lower because IRAS 250% relief doesn't cross borders — only SG-resident couples and SG-resident donors fully benefit. Definition: penetration = (couples using a dedicated wedding-charity platform in last 12 months) / (citizen marriages 2024). Source basis: estimated via Altru's own market scan + competitor public data; Giving.sg does not publish wedding-segment volume.

trends.title: Values-led / 'micro-wedding' shift among SG Gen Z and young millennial couples (2024-2027)
trends.evidence: Deloitte 2024 Gen Z & Millennial Survey (SEA): purpose-driven values rank top 3 in life choices for 28-40 cohort. Rosette Designs SG 2025 Gen Z wedding trends report: 'micro-weddings, purposeful spending, deeper meaning'. CNN Business Feb 2024: ~50% couples adjusting weddings to eliminate 'gifts and favors'. The Knot 2026 Gen Z trends report echoes US data; SG cohort lags US by ~12 months on adoption curves.
trends.implication_for_us: By 2027, charity-instead-of-cash is likely to be a recognised wedding-norm option, not a fringe choice — Altru should be the brand that owns this category in SG before incumbents (Wishlist.sg, Blissful Brides) extend into it. Action window: secure category leadership by end-2026, before Bridestory or hotel chains build in-house alternatives. Agent 4: 'category-defining brand' is a 2026 priority, not a 2027 priority.

trends.title: ASEAN cross-border QR payment integration (SG PayNow x QRIS x VietQR x QRPh) maturing 2024-2026
trends.evidence: ASEAN Integrated QR Code Payment System (2023 launch, expanding through 2025-2026); QRIS cross-border 2.6B transactions Q1 2025 (Bank Indonesia); PayNow-QRIS link active 2023; PayNow-VietQR active 2024. Settlement still has FX friction but UX-level QR scan works across SG, ID, MY, TH, VN.
trends.implication_for_us: By 2027, an Indonesian wedding guest could plausibly scan an Altru PayNow QR at a SG-hosted SG-ID couple's wedding. But: only SG-tax-resident donors get the 250% relief, so cross-border ENABLES the transaction but doesn't extend the structural advantage. Agent 4: SEA expansion is a 'distribution wedge' not a 'tax-relief wedge' — the value prop weakens outside SG. Sequence: SG saturate → SG-MY cross-border couples (where guest mix is mostly SG) → only then primary-MY market with a redesigned proposition.

trends.title: IRAS 250% IPC tax-relief sunset risk — expires 31 Dec 2026 unless renewed
trends.evidence: IRAS donations-and-tax-deductions page (current 2026-05-05): scheme extended to 31 Dec 2026 in Budget 2023. Default in absence of renewal: 100% deduction (reverts pre-2009 baseline). Budget 2025 added S$600M philanthropy push but did NOT extend 250% rate beyond 2026. Industry expectation: renewal likely but not guaranteed; legislators may also adjust the rate (e.g. 200%, 150%) rather than auto-renew.
trends.implication_for_us: This is the SINGLE largest macro risk for Altru. A reversion to 100% relief weakens the tax-math pitch by ~60% and makes Altru indistinguishable from Giving.sg + a couple-built fundraising page. Action window: by Q3 2026, lobby NCSS / Community Foundation Singapore / NVPC for sector advocacy on renewal; build a fallback messaging frame that emphasises emotional impact + matching grants + auto-receipting (the structural wedges that survive a rate cut). Agent 3 must NOT base its 3-year revenue plan solely on the 250% rate persisting.

trends.title: Hotel banquet inflation 10-20% YoY 2024-2025 — wedding budgets stretching, redirect-narrative strengthens
trends.evidence: Seedly Wedding Ang Bao Guide 2024-2025: '10-20% increase in wedding banquet packages 2024'; SingaporeBrides 2026 Banquet Price List shows 5-star hotel tables now S$2,300-2,700+ vs S$1,900-2,200 pre-COVID. Underlying drivers: F&B inflation, labour shortages in hospitality, post-pandemic demand rebound.
trends.implication_for_us: Cost-conscious couples increasingly question 'why are we spending S$100K on one night'; this opens a values-led wedge. By 2026, expect 25-35% of SG ICP couples to seriously consider non-traditional gifting structures. Agent 4: lean into the cost-conscious narrative ('your wedding doesn't have to be wasteful'); Agent 3 should track average wedding spend as a leading indicator for couple-side conversion.

trends.title: Singapore Budget 2025 SG Gives Matching Grant ramp-up 2025-2027
trends.evidence: Budget 2025 (Withers, Lexology, Mondaq): S$600M+ committed across 3 programmes including S$250M SG Gives Matching Grant; PTIS expanded coverage; Community Chest matching expanded. Implementation 2025-2027 with annual tranches.
trends.implication_for_us: By 2027, every IPC-routed donation will likely have access to some matching multiplier — this materially lifts the impact-per-dollar story. Action window: by 2026-Q4, integrate matching-multiplier logic into Altru's per-donation receipt UX and IPC partner agreements. Couple-side hero copy by 2027 should be 'your wedding unlocked S$X in matching grants and tax savings'. Agent 4: matching-multiplier amplification is a Wave 2 differentiator vs Giving.sg, which lacks personalised wedding-context matching.
